summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: What is Environmental Science?
summary_sections.content_html:
What is Environmental Science?

Environmental science is the interdisciplinary study of how humans interact with the environment. It integrates biology, chemistry, physics, geology, economics, and social sciences to understand and solve environmental problems.

Key Themes

  • Science and the environment: Using the scientific method to study natural systems.
  • Human impact: Agriculture, industry, urbanization, and resource consumption alter ecosystems.
  • Sustainability: Meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Interdisciplinary Nature of the Field
summary_sections.content_html:
Interdisciplinary Nature of the Field

Environmental science draws on multiple disciplines:

  • Biology/Ecology: Species interactions, ecosystems, biodiversity.
  • Chemistry: Pollutant behavior, biogeochemical cycles, water chemistry.
  • Physics: Energy flow, thermodynamics, climate dynamics.
  • Geology: Soil science, mineral resources, natural hazards.
  • Social Sciences: Economics (cost-benefit), policy, ethics, demographics.
  • Engineering: Pollution control, renewable energy technology, sustainable design.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Ecosystem Basics (Producers, Consumers, Decomposers)
summary_sections.content_html:
Ecosystem Basics

Trophic Levels

  • Producers (autotrophs): Photosynthesizers (plants, algae, cyanobacteria) and chemosynthesizers.
  • Primary consumers (herbivores): Eat producers (deer, grasshoppers, zooplankton).
  • Secondary consumers: Eat primary consumers (frogs, small fish).
  • Tertiary consumers: Top predators (hawks, sharks, lions).
  • Decomposers: Bacteria and fungi break down dead matter, recycling nutrients.

10% Rule: Approximately 10% of energy transfers between trophic levels; ~90% lost as heat.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Biogeochemical Cycles (Carbon, Nitrogen, Water)
summary_sections.content_html:
Biogeochemical Cycles

Carbon Cycle

Photosynthesis (removes CO₂), respiration/decomposition (releases CO₂), fossil fuel combustion, ocean absorption. Human burning of fossil fuels accelerates atmospheric CO₂ (pre-industrial ~280 ppm → 2024 ~425 ppm).

Nitrogen Cycle

N₂ (78% of atmosphere) → fixed by bacteria/lightning → NH₃ → NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻ (usable by plants) → consumed by animals → decomposition/denitrification returns N₂. Haber-Bosch process produces synthetic fertilizer.

Water Cycle

Evaporation → condensation → precipitation → runoff/infiltration → groundwater → transpiration. Human impacts: damming, irrigation, groundwater depletion, pollution.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Sustainability Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Sustainability Concepts

Three Pillars

  • Environmental: Maintaining healthy ecosystems, biodiversity, and natural resources.
  • Economic: Long-term economic viability without depleting natural capital.
  • Social: Equity, justice, health, education, and community well-being.

Key Metrics

  • Ecological footprint, carbon footprint, IPAT equation (Impact = Population × Affluence × Technology).
  • Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs): 17 UN goals for 2030.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Environmental Issues
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Environmental Issues

Dust Bowl (1930s)

Overplowing Great Plains → topsoil loss → massive dust storms. Led to soil conservation practices and the Soil Conservation Service.

Aral Sea Shrinkage

Soviet-era irrigation diverted rivers → Aral Sea shrank ~90%. Fisheries collapsed, salt/dust storms, health crises.

Ozone Hole

CFCs destroyed stratospheric ozone → UV radiation risk. Montreal Protocol (1987) phased out CFCs; ozone recovering.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Systems Thinking
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Systems Thinking

Key Concepts

  • Open vs. closed systems: Open exchange matter and energy (most ecosystems); closed exchange only energy (Earth for matter, approximately).
  • Feedback loops: Positive (amplifying: ice melt → less reflection → more warming) and negative (stabilizing: predator-prey cycles).
  • Inputs, outputs, throughputs: Energy and matter flow through systems.
  • Emergent properties: Properties arising from system interactions that individual components don't possess.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Energy Flow in Ecosystems
summary_sections.content_html:
Energy Flow in Ecosystems

Energy flows in one direction through ecosystems: Sun → producers → consumers → heat. It is not recycled.

Key Measures

  • GPP (Gross Primary Productivity): Total energy fixed by photosynthesis.
  • NPP (Net Primary Productivity): GPP minus plant respiration; energy available to consumers.
  • 1st Law of Thermodynamics: Energy is conserved — transferred, not created or destroyed.
  • 2nd Law: Energy conversions increase entropy; energy is lost as heat at each transfer.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Food Chains & Food Webs
summary_sections.content_html:
Food Chains & Food Webs

Food chain: Linear sequence of who eats whom (grass → rabbit → fox → eagle). Food web: Complex, interconnected food chains showing multiple feeding relationships in an ecosystem.

Key Concepts

  • Grazing food chain: Starts with producers (most terrestrial/aquatic systems).
  • Detrital food chain: Starts with dead organic matter (decomposition-based).
  • Keystone species: Removal causes disproportionate ecosystem change.
  • Trophic cascades: Changes at one level ripple through the web.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Population Dynamics
summary_sections.content_html:
Population Dynamics

Growth Models

  • Exponential growth: J-curve; unlimited resources; dN/dt = rN (r = birth rate − death rate).
  • Logistic growth: S-curve; limited resources; dN/dt = rN(1 − N/K) where K = carrying capacity.

Strategies

  • r-selected: Many offspring, little parental care, rapid growth (bacteria, insects).
  • K-selected: Few offspring, extensive parental care, slow growth (elephants, whales).

Regulation

Density-dependent (competition, predation, disease) and density-independent (weather, fire, flood) factors.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Community Interactions (Competition, Symbiosis)
summary_sections.content_html:
Community Interactions

Types

  • Competition: Interspecific (between species) and intraspecific (within species). Competitive exclusion principle — two species can't occupy the same niche indefinitely.
  • Predation: One organism kills and eats another; drives coevolution (camouflage, warning coloration).
  • Symbiosis: Mutualism (+/+), commensalism (+/0), parasitism (+/−).
  • Niche partitioning: Species reduce competition by using different parts of the same resource.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Succession (Primary & Secondary)
summary_sections.content_html:
Ecological Succession

Primary Succession

Occurs on bare substrate with no soil (lava flows, retreating glaciers). Pioneer species (lichens, mosses) colonize first → soil develops → grasses → shrubs → trees → climax community. Takes centuries to millennia.

Secondary Succession

Occurs after disturbance that leaves soil intact (fire, logging, abandonment). Faster than primary — soil, seeds, and organisms remain. Grasses/weeds → shrubs → trees → climax community.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Biodiversity & Conservation
summary_sections.content_html:
Biodiversity & Conservation

Levels of Biodiversity

  • Genetic: Variation within a species (allele diversity).
  • Species: Number of different species in an area.
  • Ecosystem: Variety of habitats, communities, and ecological processes.

Threats

HIPPCO: Habitat destruction, Invasive species, Population growth, Pollution, Climate change, Overexploitation. Currently in the 6th mass extinction (species loss rate 100–1,000× background).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Ecology
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Ecology

Yellowstone Wolf Reintroduction (1995)

Wolves reintroduced → elk behavior changed → riparian vegetation recovered → streambank stability → beaver return → trophic cascade demonstrating top-down regulation.

Coral Reef Decline

Warming → coral bleaching (loss of zooxanthellae) → biodiversity collapse. 14% of world's coral lost 2009–2018.

Amazon Deforestation

~17% of Amazon cleared for cattle and soy; approaching tipping point where forest could convert to savanna, releasing massive CO₂.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Population Models
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Population Models

Calculations

  • Growth rate (r): r = (births − deaths) / N or r = birth rate − death rate.
  • Doubling time: t = 70/r (Rule of 70); r in percent.
  • Exponential: dN/dt = rN.
  • Logistic: dN/dt = rN(1 − N/K). Maximum growth at N = K/2.
  • Population change: ΔN = (births + immigration) − (deaths + emigration).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Human Population Growth
summary_sections.content_html:
Human Population Growth

World population ~8 billion (2024). Grew slowly for millennia then exponentially after the Industrial Revolution (better agriculture, sanitation, medicine). Current growth rate ~1.0%/yr.

Key Milestones

  • 1 billion: ~1800
  • 2 billion: 1927
  • 4 billion: 1974
  • 8 billion: 2022

Total Fertility Rate (TFR): Average children per woman. Replacement level ≈ 2.1. TFR varies: Sub-Saharan Africa ~4.5; Europe ~1.5.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Demographic Transition Model
summary_sections.content_html:
Demographic Transition Model (DTM)

Four Stages

  • Stage 1 – Pre-industrial: High birth and death rates → slow/no growth.
  • Stage 2 – Transitional: Death rates drop (better health care); birth rates remain high → rapid growth.
  • Stage 3 – Industrial: Birth rates decline (urbanization, education, family planning) → growth slows.
  • Stage 4 – Post-industrial: Low birth and death rates → slow/zero growth.

Some add Stage 5: Birth rate falls below death rate → population decline (Japan, parts of Europe).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Age Structure Diagrams
summary_sections.content_html:
Age Structure Diagrams

Population pyramids show age and sex distribution. Shape predicts future growth:

  • Expansive (wide base): Rapid growth — many young people (Nigeria, Ethiopia).
  • Stationary (column-shaped): Stable — roughly equal numbers at each age (US, Australia).
  • Constrictive (narrow base): Declining — fewer young than middle-aged (Japan, Germany, Italy).

Diagrams reveal dependency ratio and demographic momentum — critical for policy planning.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Carrying Capacity
summary_sections.content_html:
Carrying Capacity & Human Population

Earth's carrying capacity for humans is debated. Estimates range from 4 to 16 billion depending on consumption levels and technology.

Key Concepts

  • Ecological footprint: Area of productive land/water needed to sustain a person's lifestyle.
  • Overshoot: Humanity currently uses ~1.7 Earths' worth of resources per year.
  • Earth Overshoot Day: Date humanity has used more resources than Earth can regenerate that year (moves earlier each decade).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Urbanization & Land Use
summary_sections.content_html:
Urbanization & Land Use

Over 56% of the world's population lives in cities (projected 68% by 2050). Urbanization concentrates consumption but can also create efficiencies.

Impacts

  • Urban heat island: Cities are warmer than surroundings (concrete + less vegetation + waste heat).
  • Sprawl: Low-density, car-dependent expansion converts farmland/habitat.
  • Smart growth: Compact, mixed-use, transit-oriented development to reduce sprawl.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies: Population Policy
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies: Population Policy

China's One-Child Policy (1980–2015)

Reduced TFR from ~6 to ~1.7. Now facing aging crisis; shifted to two-child (2016) then three-child (2021) policy.

India

Voluntary family planning, women's education. TFR dropped from ~6 (1960) to ~2.0 (2023). Now world's most populous country.

Kerala Model

Indian state achieved low TFR (~1.6) through high female literacy (97%), good healthcare, without coercion.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Demographic Calculations
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Demographic Calculations

Key Formulas

  • Growth rate (r): r = (CBR − CDR) / 10 (gives %; CBR and CDR per 1,000).
  • Doubling time: t = 70 / r (Rule of 70).
  • Rate of natural increase: (births − deaths) / total population × 100.
  • Net migration rate: (immigration − emigration) / population × 1,000.
  • Crude birth rate / Crude death rate: births or deaths per 1,000 people per year.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Renewable vs. Nonrenewable Resources
summary_sections.content_html:
Renewable vs. Nonrenewable Resources

Renewable: Replenished on a human timescale — solar, wind, water, biomass, geothermal. Sustainable if used at or below replacement rate.

Nonrenewable: Formed over millions of years — fossil fuels, minerals, metals. Finite; extraction degrades the environment.

Key Distinction

Even renewables can become depleted if overexploited (e.g., overfishing, deforestation faster than regrowth).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Water Resources & Management
summary_sections.content_html:
Water Resources & Management

Only ~2.5% of Earth's water is fresh; ~69% of that is in glaciers/ice caps. Available fresh water (<1%) comes from groundwater, rivers, and lakes.

Key Issues

  • Aquifer depletion: Ogallala Aquifer depleting faster than recharge (irrigation in Great Plains).
  • Water pollution: Point source (factory pipe) vs. Non-point source (agricultural runoff).
  • Water stress: ~4 billion people face severe water scarcity at least one month per year.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Soil & Agriculture
summary_sections.content_html:
Soil & Agriculture

Soil forms from weathered rock + organic matter over centuries. Soil horizons: O (organic litter), A (topsoil), B (subsoil), C (weathered parent material), R (bedrock).

Agricultural Impacts

  • Erosion: Wind and water remove topsoil; ~24 billion tons lost annually.
  • Salinization: Salt buildup from irrigation in arid regions.
  • Green Revolution: High-yield crops + fertilizers + irrigation; fed billions but increased environmental costs.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Forests & Deforestation
summary_sections.content_html:
Forests & Deforestation

Forests cover ~31% of Earth's land. They provide ecosystem services: carbon storage, biodiversity habitat, water cycling, soil protection, timber, and recreation.

Deforestation

~10 million hectares/year lost (2015–2020). Main drivers: cattle ranching, soy/palm oil farming, logging, and urbanization. Tropical forests are most threatened.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Fisheries & Ocean Resources
summary_sections.content_html:
Fisheries & Ocean Resources

Fish provide ~17% of animal protein consumed globally. ~34% of fish stocks are overfished; ~60% are fished at maximum sustained yield.

Key Concepts

  • Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY): Largest catch that can be taken indefinitely.
  • Bycatch: Unintended species caught and often discarded (dolphins, turtles, sharks).
  • Aquaculture: Farmed fish; now supplies >50% of fish consumed but has environmental impacts.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies: Resource Use
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies: Resource Use

Aral Sea Disaster

Soviet irrigation diverted rivers feeding the Aral Sea → lost 90% of volume by 2010. Desertification, salt storms, fishery collapse, health crises. Partial recovery in North Aral with dam.

Three Gorges Dam (China)

World's largest hydroelectric dam. Generates ~100 TWh/year. But: displaced 1.3 million people, causes erosion, sedimentation, and reduced biodiversity downstream.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Resource Management Models
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Resource Management Models

Key Models & Concepts

  • Tragedy of the commons: Shared resources depleted by self-interest (Hardin, 1968). Solutions: regulation, privatization, community management.
  • Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY): Largest harvest sustainable indefinitely. Applied to fisheries, forestry.
  • Cost-benefit analysis: Weighing economic costs vs. benefits of a resource decision; externalities often excluded.
  • Precautionary principle: When uncertain, err on the side of protection.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Fossil Fuels
summary_sections.content_html:
Fossil Fuels

Coal, oil (petroleum), and natural gas supply ~80% of global energy. Formed from ancient organic matter over millions of years.

Impacts

  • CO₂ emissions: Leading cause of climate change.
  • Air pollution: SO₂, NOₓ, particulates → smog, acid rain, respiratory illness.
  • Extraction damage: Mountaintop removal, oil spills, fracking (hydrofracturing) → water contamination.

Coal is dirtiest per unit energy; natural gas cleanest fossil fuel (~50% less CO₂ than coal). Oil used mostly for transportation.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Nuclear Energy
summary_sections.content_html:
Nuclear Energy

Fission: Splitting heavy atoms (uranium-235, plutonium-239) releases enormous energy. Provides ~10% of global electricity with near-zero CO₂ during operation.

Pros & Cons

  • Pros: High energy density, low carbon, reliable baseload power.
  • Cons: Radioactive waste (remains hazardous for thousands of years), meltdown risk (Chernobyl, Fukushima), high construction costs, uranium mining impacts.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Renewable Energy (Solar, Wind, Hydro, Geothermal)
summary_sections.content_html:
Renewable Energy Sources

Solar

Photovoltaic (PV) cells convert sunlight directly to electricity. Fastest-growing energy source; costs dropped ~90% since 2010.

Wind

Turbines convert kinetic energy of wind. 2nd-fastest growing; concerns include bird/bat mortality and visual impact.

Hydroelectric

Largest renewable source (~16% of global electricity). Dams displace communities and alter ecosystems.

Geothermal

Heat from Earth's interior. Limited to tectonically active areas but very reliable.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Energy Efficiency & Conservation
summary_sections.content_html:
Energy Efficiency & Conservation

Efficiency: Getting more useful work from less energy input. Conservation: Using less energy through behavioral changes.

Key Strategies

  • LED lighting uses ~75% less energy than incandescent.
  • Building insulation, efficient appliances, ENERGY STAR standards.
  • Cogeneration (CHP): Uses waste heat from electricity generation for heating (~80% efficiency vs. ~33% for conventional plants).
  • Transportation: EVs, public transit, fuel economy standards.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies: Energy Policy
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies: Energy Policy

Germany's Energiewende

Ambitious transition to renewables. ~50% of electricity from renewables (2023). Also phased out nuclear. Higher costs; criticized for coal persistence during transition.

Iceland

~100% of electricity from renewables (geothermal + hydro), thanks to abundant volcanic activity. Model for renewable-powered society.

US Energy Policy

Mix of federal/state policies: clean energy tax credits, CAFE standards, state renewable portfolio standards. Inflation Reduction Act (2022) largest US climate investment.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Energy Calculations
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Energy Calculations

Key Formulas

  • Efficiency: η = (useful energy output / total energy input) × 100%
  • Power: P = Energy / Time (watts = joules/seconds)
  • Electrical energy: E = P × t (kWh = kW × hours)
  • Cost: Cost = kWh × price per kWh
  • 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Air Pollution
summary_sections.content_html:
Air Pollution

Primary Pollutants

Emitted directly: CO, SO₂, NOₓ, particulate matter (PM), volatile organic compounds (VOCs), lead.

Secondary Pollutants

Formed by chemical reactions in the atmosphere: ozone (O₃), smog, acid rain.

Key Issues

  • Criteria pollutants: 6 regulated by the Clean Air Act (CO, Pb, NO₂, O₃, PM, SO₂).
  • Indoor air pollution: Radon, VOCs, mold, CO — kills ~3.8 million/year globally (cooking with solid fuels).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Water Pollution
summary_sections.content_html:
Water Pollution

Types

  • Point source: Single identifiable source (factory pipe, sewage outfall).
  • Non-point source: Diffuse (agricultural runoff, urban stormwater).

Major Pollutants

  • Nutrients: N, P → eutrophication → algal blooms → dead zones.
  • Pathogens: Bacteria, viruses from sewage → cholera, dysentery.
  • Toxics: Heavy metals (Hg, Pb), pesticides, pharmaceuticals.
  • Thermal: Heated discharge from power plants reduces dissolved O₂.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Soil Pollution & Land Degradation
summary_sections.content_html:
Soil Pollution & Land Degradation

Soil contamination comes from industrial waste, pesticides, mining, landfills, and atmospheric deposition. Land degradation affects ~25% of Earth's ice-free land.

Key Issues

  • Brownfields: Former industrial sites with contamination requiring cleanup.
  • Superfund (CERCLA): US law funding cleanup of the worst hazardous waste sites.
  • Bioremediation: Using organisms to break down contaminants (bacteria, fungi, plants).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Solid Waste Management
summary_sections.content_html:
Solid Waste Management

The US generates ~292 million tons of municipal solid waste (MSW) annually (~4.9 lbs/person/day). Globally: ~2 billion tons/year.

Hierarchy (most to least preferred)

  • Reduce → Reuse → Recycle/Compost → Energy recovery → Landfill

US recycles ~32% of MSW. Landfills remain the primary disposal method despite methane emissions.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Hazardous Waste & Recycling
summary_sections.content_html:
Hazardous Waste & Recycling

Hazardous waste: Waste that is ignitable, corrosive, reactive, or toxic. Regulated by RCRA (Resource Conservation and Recovery Act, 1976).

Recycling

Reduces need for virgin resources, saves energy (recycling aluminum uses 95% less energy than new), and reduces landfill burden. Challenges: contamination, market economics, single-stream confusion.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies: Pollution Control
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies: Pollution Control

US Acid Rain Program (1990 Clean Air Act Amendments)

Cap-and-trade for SO₂ emissions. Reduced SO₂ by ~90% since 1990. Cost far less than predicted. Model for pollution regulation.

London Smog (1952)

Great Smog killed ~12,000 people. Led to UK's Clean Air Act (1956). Spurred global air quality awareness.

Flint Water Crisis (2014)

Water source switch corroded lead pipes → lead contamination. Disproportionately affected low-income, majority-Black community. Environmental justice failure.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Pollution Models
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Pollution Models

Key Concepts for Calculations

  • LD50: Dose lethal to 50% of test organisms; measures acute toxicity.
  • Dose-response curve: Plots health effect vs. exposure dose; determines threshold and no-observed-adverse-effect level (NOAEL).
  • Bioaccumulation factor: Concentration in organism / concentration in environment.
  • Biomagnification factor: Concentration at higher trophic level / concentration at lower level.
  • Half-life: Time for pollutant concentration to decrease by half.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Greenhouse Effect & Global Warming
summary_sections.content_html:
Greenhouse Effect & Global Warming

The natural greenhouse effect keeps Earth ~33°C warmer than it would be without an atmosphere (−18°C → +15°C). Greenhouse gases (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, H₂O, CFCs) absorb and re-emit infrared radiation.

Enhanced Greenhouse Effect

Human activities (fossil fuels, deforestation, agriculture) have increased CO₂ from ~280 ppm (pre-industrial) to ~424 ppm (2024). Global temperature has risen ~1.1°C above pre-industrial levels.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Evidence of Climate Change
summary_sections.content_html:
Evidence of Climate Change

Multiple Lines of Evidence

  • Temperature records: Instrumental records since 1850; 10 warmest years all since 2010.
  • Ice cores: Trapped air bubbles show CO₂/temperature correlation over 800,000 years.
  • Sea level rise: ~20 cm since 1900; accelerating (~3.6 mm/year now).
  • Arctic ice decline: Summer Arctic sea ice volume down ~75% since 1979.
  • Ocean warming & acidification: Oceans absorbed ~90% of excess heat and ~30% of CO₂.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Impacts on Ecosystems & Humans
summary_sections.content_html:
Climate Change Impacts

Ecosystems

  • Range shifts: species moving poleward and to higher elevations.
  • Coral bleaching: 14% of coral lost 2009–2018.
  • Phenology disruption: mismatches between species (pollinators arriving at wrong time).

Humans

  • Extreme weather: more intense heat waves, droughts, hurricanes, and floods.
  • Food security: crop yield declines; fisheries shifting.
  • Health: heat-related illness, vector-borne diseases expanding range, air quality worsening.
  • Climate refugees: displacement from sea level rise, drought, and extreme weather.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Mitigation & Adaptation
summary_sections.content_html:
Mitigation & Adaptation

Mitigation (reducing causes)

  • Transition to renewable energy.
  • Energy efficiency improvements.
  • Carbon pricing (tax or cap-and-trade).
  • Reforestation and soil carbon sequestration.

Adaptation (adjusting to impacts)

  • Sea walls and flood barriers.
  • Drought-resistant crops.
  • Urban cooling (green infrastructure, reflective surfaces).
  • Early warning systems for extreme weather.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: International Agreements (Kyoto, Paris)
summary_sections.content_html:
International Climate Agreements

Kyoto Protocol (1997)

First binding agreement to reduce GHG emissions. Required developed nations to reduce emissions ~5% below 1990 levels. US never ratified. Mixed results.

Paris Agreement (2015)

Nearly every nation (196 parties). Goal: limit warming to well below 2°C, preferably 1.5°C. Countries set own targets (NDCs — Nationally Determined Contributions). Not legally binding. Updated every 5 years.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies: Climate Policy
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies: Climate Policy

EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS)

World's largest carbon market. Cap-and-trade covering ~40% of EU emissions. Reduced covered emissions ~35% since 2005.

California's Climate Programs

State-level action: cap-and-trade, 100% clean electricity by 2045, EV mandates, building codes. Often ahead of federal policy.

China's Dual Role

Largest emitter (~30% of global CO₂) but also largest renewable energy investor. Launched world's largest ETS in 2021.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Climate Models
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Climate Models

Key Concepts

  • Climate models (GCMs): Computer simulations of Earth's climate system using physics equations. Multiple models run for different emission scenarios.
  • Emission scenarios (RCPs/SSPs): RCP 2.6 (aggressive mitigation) to RCP 8.5 (business as usual). SSPs update these.
  • Model outputs: Temperature projections range from +1.5°C to +4.5°C+ by 2100 depending on emissions.
  • Carbon budget: Total CO₂ that can still be emitted to stay below a temperature target (~400 Gt CO₂ remaining for 1.5°C).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: History of Environmental Movements
summary_sections.content_html:
History of Environmental Movements

Modern environmentalism grew from conservation and preservation movements of the 19th century.

Key Milestones

  • 1850s–1900s: John Muir (preservation), Gifford Pinchot (conservation/wise use), creation of first national parks.
  • 1962: Rachel Carson's Silent Spring — exposed pesticide dangers, sparked modern environmental movement.
  • 1970: First Earth Day (20 million participants). EPA established. Clean Air Act, Clean Water Act followed.
  • 1980s–present: Environmental justice movement, global issues (ozone, climate), sustainability focus.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Environmental Laws & Regulations
summary_sections.content_html:
Major US Environmental Laws

  • NEPA (1970): Requires Environmental Impact Statements (EIS) for federal projects.
  • Clean Air Act (1970, amended 1990): Regulates air pollutants; NAAQS standards; cap-and-trade for SO₂.
  • Clean Water Act (1972): Regulates point-source water pollution; NPDES permits; goal: fishable, swimmable waters.
  • Endangered Species Act (1973): Protects listed species and their critical habitat.
  • RCRA (1976): Regulates hazardous waste from generation to disposal (cradle-to-grave).
  • CERCLA/Superfund (1980): Cleans up contaminated sites; 'polluter pays' principle.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: International Environmental Policy
summary_sections.content_html:
International Environmental Policy

Key Agreements

  • Stockholm Conference (1972): First major UN conference on the environment; established UNEP.
  • CITES (1975): Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species — regulates wildlife trade.
  • Montreal Protocol (1987): Phased out CFCs; ozone layer recovering — most successful environmental treaty.
  • CBD (1992): Convention on Biological Diversity — conservation and sustainable use of biodiversity.
  • Kunming-Montreal Framework (2022): '30 by 30' — protect 30% of land and sea by 2030.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Environmental Justice
summary_sections.content_html:
Environmental Justice

Environmental justice is the fair treatment and meaningful involvement of all people regardless of race, color, income, or national origin with respect to environmental laws, regulations, and policies.

Key Issues

  • Hazardous waste facilities disproportionately sited in minority and low-income communities.
  • Flint, MI water crisis (2014): lead contamination from changed water source; primarily affecting Black residents.
  • Cancer Alley, Louisiana: 85-mile industrial corridor with elevated cancer rates in Black communities.
  • Executive Order 12898 (1994): required agencies to address environmental justice.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies: Policy Implementation
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies: Policy Implementation

Success: Acid Rain Program

Clean Air Act cap-and-trade reduced SO₂ emissions ~90% from 1990 levels — ahead of schedule and under budget. Lakes and forests recovering.

Ongoing Challenge: Superfund

Over 1,300 sites on the National Priorities List. Cleanup slow and expensive; ~400 sites fully cleaned since 1980. Environmental justice concerns.

Controversial: ESA Implementation

Spotted owl vs. logging in Pacific Northwest. Species recovery success stories but habitat-economy conflicts persist.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Policy Analysis
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Policy Analysis

FRQ Strategy

  • Identify the problem: What environmental issue is being addressed?
  • Describe the policy: What does the law/regulation do? Who enforces it?
  • Evaluate effectiveness: Has it achieved its goals? What evidence?
  • Analyze trade-offs: Economic costs vs. environmental/health benefits.
  • Propose improvements: How could the policy be strengthened or reformed?

Key Comparisons

Command-and-control vs. market-based approaches. Federal vs. state vs. international. Point source vs. non-point source. Mitigation vs. adaptation.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Principles of Sustainability
summary_sections.content_html:
Principles of Sustainability

Sustainability means meeting the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs (Brundtland Commission, 1987).

Three Pillars

  • Environmental: Maintaining ecosystem health and biodiversity.
  • Economic: Supporting livelihoods and economic viability long-term.
  • Social: Ensuring equity, well-being, and community resilience.

Key Frameworks

UN Sustainable Development Goals (17 SDGs, 2015–2030). Ecological footprint analysis. Circular economy.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Sustainable Agriculture
summary_sections.content_html:
Sustainable Agriculture

Problems with Industrial Agriculture

  • Soil degradation, erosion, and loss of topsoil.
  • Water pollution from fertilizer runoff (dead zones).
  • Pesticide impacts on pollinators and human health.
  • ~25% of global greenhouse gas emissions from food systems.

Sustainable Practices

  • Crop rotation and cover crops: Maintain soil health, reduce erosion.
  • Integrated Pest Management (IPM): Minimize pesticide use through biological and cultural controls.
  • No-till farming: Reduces soil disturbance, maintains carbon in soil.
  • Agroforestry: Integrating trees with crops and livestock.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Sustainable Energy
summary_sections.content_html:
Sustainable Energy

Transition to Renewables

  • Solar: Fastest-growing energy source; costs dropped ~90% since 2010.
  • Wind: Competitive with fossil fuels; onshore and expanding offshore.
  • Hydropower: Largest renewable source but limited growth potential and ecological impacts.
  • Energy storage: Battery technology (lithium-ion) crucial for variable renewables.

Energy Efficiency

The cheapest and cleanest energy is the energy we don't use. Building standards, LED lighting, efficient appliances, and industrial optimization can reduce demand 30-50%.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Green Building & Urban Planning
summary_sections.content_html:
Green Building & Urban Planning

Sustainable Buildings

  • Energy-efficient design: passive solar, insulation, LED lighting, smart systems.
  • Green materials: recycled content, sustainably sourced wood, low-VOC paints.
  • LEED certification: Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design.

Sustainable Urban Planning

  • Smart growth: Compact, mixed-use development reducing sprawl.
  • Public transit: Reducing car dependence and emissions.
  • Green infrastructure: Parks, green roofs, bioswales, urban forests for cooling and stormwater.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies: Sustainable Practices
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies: Sustainable Practices

Freiburg, Germany — Green City

Solar capital of Europe. 40% of trips by bike or transit. Vauban district: car-free, passive solar housing, district heating.

Costa Rica — Conservation Leader

~99% electricity from renewables. Reversed deforestation (forest cover 25% → 52%). Payment for Ecosystem Services (PES) program.

Bhutan — Gross National Happiness

Carbon-negative country. Measures progress by well-being, not GDP. Constitutional mandate: 60%+ forest cover forever.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Sustainability Metrics
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Sustainability Metrics

Key Metrics

  • Ecological footprint: Demand on ecosystems in global hectares (gha).
  • Carbon footprint: Total GHG emissions from an activity, product, or entity.
  • IPAT equation: Impact = Population × Affluence × Technology.
  • HDI (Human Development Index): Life expectancy + education + income → well-being measure.
  • Genuine Progress Indicator: GDP adjusted for environmental and social costs.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Review: Key Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Review: Key Concepts Across Environmental Science

Core Themes

  • Ecosystems & Biodiversity: Energy flow, nutrient cycling, species interactions, biomes, biodiversity value.
  • Population & Resources: Carrying capacity, demographic transition, renewable vs. nonrenewable resources.
  • Pollution & Health: Air, water, soil pollution sources and effects; bioaccumulation; environmental health.
  • Climate Change: Greenhouse effect, evidence, impacts, mitigation, adaptation, international agreements.
  • Sustainability: Three pillars, IPAT, ecological footprint, sustainable practices.
  • Policy & Law: Major US laws, international treaties, environmental justice.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP-Style Practice
summary_sections.content_html:
AP-Style Practice Questions

This lesson focuses on practice with the types of questions found on the AP Environmental Science (APES) exam: multiple choice and free-response, covering all major topics.

Exam Format

  • 80 multiple-choice questions (90 minutes) — 60% of score.
  • 3 free-response questions (70 minutes) — 40% of score.
  • FRQ types: Design an Investigation, Analyze an Environmental Problem, Propose a Solution.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies: AP APES
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies for AP APES

The AP exam frequently uses case studies to test application of environmental concepts to real-world situations.

Classic APES Case Studies

  • Chesapeake Bay: Nutrient pollution, dead zones, multi-state management.
  • Aral Sea: Water diversion for irrigation → ecological disaster.
  • Easter Island: Resource depletion and societal collapse.
  • Three Gorges Dam: Hydropower benefits vs. displacement and ecological costs.
  • Ogallala Aquifer: Groundwater depletion from agricultural irrigation.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Environmental Science: Real-World Applications

Connecting Science to Action

  • Personal choices: Energy use, transportation, diet, consumption, waste reduction.
  • Community action: Local environmental organizations, citizen science, city council advocacy.
  • Career paths: Environmental engineering, conservation biology, policy analysis, urban planning, renewable energy, environmental law.
  • Technology solutions: Remote sensing, GIS mapping, water treatment, carbon capture, precision agriculture.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Capstone: Environmental Impact Study
summary_sections.content_html:
Capstone: Environmental Impact Study

This lesson guides students through conducting their own environmental impact analysis, integrating concepts from all units.

Steps for an Environmental Impact Study

  • Step 1: Define the project or action being assessed.
  • Step 2: Identify affected environmental components (air, water, soil, biodiversity, communities).
  • Step 3: Predict impacts (positive and negative, short-term and long-term).
  • Step 4: Evaluate significance and propose mitigation measures.
  • Step 5: Consider alternatives, including the 'no-action' alternative.
  • Step 6: Communicate findings with data, evidence, and recommendations.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Comprehensive Review & AP Prep
summary_sections.content_html:
Comprehensive Review & AP Prep

Final Review Strategy

  • Content mastery: Review key concepts from all 9 units systematically.
  • Connections: Practice linking topics (e.g., agriculture → water pollution → dead zones → policy).
  • Calculations: Practice IPAT, percent change, per capita, Rule of 70, energy calculations.
  • FRQ practice: Write timed responses using Claim-Evidence-Reasoning format.
  • Vocabulary: Ensure all key terms can be defined AND applied in context.

Top 10 APES Topics

Energy, water resources, air/water pollution, climate change, biodiversity, soil/agriculture, population, environmental law, sustainability, and environmental justice.
